summary: Gruber follows up on his previous analysis of Steve Ballmer's comments about iPod users and stolen music. After controversy arose about whether Ballmer's quotes were taken out of context by Silicon.com, Gruber examines a fuller transcription published by The Guardian Unlimited. He concludes that even with additional context, Ballmer clearly claimed most music on iPods is stolen and implied that support for non-DRM music is a problem. Gruber argues this reveals Ballmer fundamentally misunderstands why the iPod is popular.
key_points: Silicon.com had to retitle their article after misquoting Ballmer with fabricated direct quotes, though the substance remained accurate
Even with full context restored, Ballmer's core claim that most iPod music is stolen holds up as reported
Ballmer frames the iPod's support for non-DRM music as a 'problem' rather than a feature
Gruber argues Ballmer's DRM-centric worldview blinds him to understanding the iPod's actual appeal to consumers
money_quotes: Ballmer clearly claimed that most music on iPods is "stolen", and implies that the iPod's support for non-DRM-protected music is a "problem".
If this is really what he believes, he has no idea why the iPod is so amazingly popular.
The fact that you can buy it and it's protected doesn't affect the fact that most people still steal music.
As long as there's alternate forms of music acquisition, there still will be ways for people to steal music.
key_insight: Even with full context restored, Ballmer's comments reveal Microsoft's fundamental misunderstanding of why consumers love the iPod — it's not about DRM, it's about the user experience.
